---
title: "Front Matter"
---

::: {.archive-notice}
**Source:** Pages 1--16 of *MintonThesis.pdf* (September 2009). Text extracted from PDF; figures extracted directly as images.
:::

University of York

Work and its Other: A Multipleperspective Analysis of Incapacity
Benefit Reform

Jonathan William Minton
Department of Sociology
July 2009

Abstract

This is a thesis of two halves: five „theory‟ chapters and five „empirical‟ chapters,
prefaced by an introduction, and then followed by a conclusion.
The five „empirical‟ chapters are based around understanding the development and rollout of a programme of Incapacity Benefits reform known as „Pathways to Work‟, which
was first introduced in 2002. Pathways to Work has since been implemented in all
Jobcentre Plus offices across the UK, and so this half of the thesis could be seen as a
case study of a „successfully implemented‟ government policy.
The five „theoretical‟ chapters introduce, explain, and interpret a range of theories about
social processes that operate at various temporal and spatial scales. These theories range
from the very small scale (human neurobiology) to the very large scale (archaeology
and biogeography), and conclude with mid-scale theories relating to observed and
intuitive everyday interpersonal interactions.
The two halves of the thesis are intended to be complementary, with the „theoretical‟
half illuminating the „empirical‟ half and the „empirical‟ half illuminating the
„theoretical‟ half. The thesis concludes by offering a number of policy suggestions, and
making the complementarity between thesis halves more explicit.


List of Contents
List of Contents ................................................................................................................. 3
List of Figures ................................................................................................................. 10
List of Tables ................................................................................................................... 14
Acknowledgements ......................................................................................................... 15
Author‟s Declaration ....................................................................................................... 16


Introduction .............................................................................................................. 17
1.1

Theory Section .................................................................................................. 18

1.2

Evidence Section .............................................................................................. 20

1.3

Summary and Conclusion................................................................................. 21

Organisms, Decisions and Emotions: Some Theories of Very Small Scale Social

Phenomena ...................................................................................................................... 23


2.1

Introduction ...................................................................................................... 23

2.2

Health Inequalities and Human Physiology ..................................................... 24

2.3

The Individual and the Organism ..................................................................... 26

2.4

Organisms and Boundaries ............................................................................... 28

2.5

Organisms as Abstract Machines ..................................................................... 29

2.6

Objects and Emotions ....................................................................................... 30

2.7

Feelings and Self .............................................................................................. 31

2.8

Centralisation and the Proto-self ...................................................................... 32

2.9

Emotions and Decision-making ....................................................................... 32

2.10

The As-if-body-loop ......................................................................................... 34

2.11

From Memories and Dispositions to Habits and Habitus ................................. 34

2.12

Conclusion: Sketching an Abstract Machine ................................................... 37

Empires, Elites and States: Some Theories of Very Large Scale Social Phenomena
40
3.1

Introduction ...................................................................................................... 40

3.2

Evolution and Social Processes ........................................................................ 41

3.3

Food, Social Structure, and Societal Complexity ............................................. 41

3.4

Stages of Societal Complexity.......................................................................... 45

3.5

Societal Complexity and Environmental Carrying Capacity ........................... 48

3.6

Types of Social Problems ................................................................................. 50

3.6.1

Internal Security Problems: The problem of conflict between unrelated

strangers 50
3.6.2

Internal Logistical Problems: The problem of transferring goods between

members51
3.6.3

Internal Logistical Problems: Societal Decision Making .......................... 53

3.6.4

External Security Issues: Threat from other states .................................... 53

3.7

Kleptocracy and the Growth of Inequality ....................................................... 55

Obedience and Hierarchy: Some Theories of Small Scale Social Phenomena ....... 59
4.1

Introduction ...................................................................................................... 59

4.2

Description ....................................................................................................... 61

4.3

The Break-point ................................................................................................ 63

4.4

Predictions ........................................................................................................ 63

4.5

Modes of Response ........................................................................................... 65

4.5.1


Performative Morality ............................................................................... 67

4.6

Variations ......................................................................................................... 67

4.7

Summary & Discussion .................................................................................... 74

Bureaucracies and Organisations: Some Theories of Large Scale Social Phenomena
76
5.1

Introduction ...................................................................................................... 76

5.2

Weber‟s Anatomy of Bureaucracy ................................................................... 77

5.3

Durable Multi-level Hierarchies ....................................................................... 78

5.3.1

Types of Authority .................................................................................... 81

5.4

Modernity: the transition from traditional to legal rule .................................... 82

5.5

Functionaries and Roles ................................................................................... 85

5.6

Writing, Law and Literacy................................................................................ 90

5.6.1
5.7

Written Documents as Symbolic Authority Figures ................................. 93

The Self-sustaining Qualities of Bureaucratic Structures ................................ 97

Some theories of observable social phenomena and their implications as

socioeconomic mechanisms .......................................................................................... 101


6.1

Introduction .................................................................................................... 101

6.2

Relational Models Theory .............................................................................. 103

6.3

Mode 1: Communal Sharing (CS) .................................................................. 104

6.4

Mode 2: Authority Ranking, and Mode 3: Equality Matching....................... 105

6.5

Mode 4: Market Pricing.................................................................................. 107

6.6

Substantivism ................................................................................................. 109

6.7

Conclusion: Bureaucracies, Metrics, and Modes ........................................... 110

The Emergence of the Work-As-Employment and State-As-Providers Norms .... 112
7.1

Introduction .................................................................................................... 112

7.2

A Cross-sectional Slice through Contemporary Economic Categorisation.... 112

7.2.1

Technical Documents as Evidence .......................................................... 113

7.2.2

Analysis of Evidence ............................................................................... 121

7.3

Longitudinal

Considerations:

The

Historical

Origins

of

Recorded

Unemployment .......................................................................................................... 127
7.3.1

The Use of Union Records ...................................................................... 127

7.3.2

Historical Context and „General Employment‟ ....................................... 128

7.3.3

Centralisation and Adaptation ................................................................. 129

7.4

7.4.1

Epistemic Poverty ................................................................................... 132

7.4.2

Gaming the System ................................................................................. 132

7.5

Two Concepts: „Epistemic Poverty‟ and „Gaming the System‟ ..................... 131

Concluding Remarks ...................................................................................... 134

Invalidity Benefit and Incapacity Benefit .............................................................. 135
8.1

Introduction .................................................................................................... 135

8.2

On the Conditions Under Which the Qualities of Government Benefit (And

Related) Statistics Arose............................................................................................ 135

8.3

Longitudinal Investigations ............................................................................ 137

8.3.1

Sickness and Unemployment Benefits: Some comparisons ................... 139

8.3.2

Exploring Longitudinal Patterns in More Detail ..................................... 140

8.3.3

Longitudinal Comparisons between IVB/IB and other Longitudinal

Statistics 141
8.3.4

IVB/IB and Working Age Population ..................................................... 142

8.3.5

IVB/IB and Female Working Age Economic Activity ........................... 143

8.3.6

Cohort Effects and Social Class .............................................................. 146

8.4

On the relationship between sickness, employment, and social class amongst

males 148
8.4.1

Introduction and Summary ...................................................................... 148

8.4.2

Replication .............................................................................................. 149

8.4.3

Correlations ............................................................................................. 154

8.4.4

A Graphical Illustration ........................................................................... 156

8.5

Cross-Sectional Trends and Characteristics ................................................... 157

8.5.1

Introduction ............................................................................................. 157

8.5.2

„Reasons for Inactivity‟ ........................................................................... 158

8.5.3

On the relationship between incapacity benefit claimant levels and state

retirement age......................................................................................................... 159
8.6

Summary and Discussion ............................................................................... 161

Pathways to Work: Qualitative Descriptions ......................................................... 163
9.1

Introduction .................................................................................................... 163

9.2

What is Pathways to Work?............................................................................ 164

9.3

Pathways to Work: Plans and Pilot Schemes ................................................. 165

9.4

Key Elements of Pathways to Work ............................................................... 167

9.5

Incapacity Benefit Personal Advisers (IBPAs) .............................................. 171

9.6

Work-Focussed Interviews (WFIs) ................................................................ 176

9.7

The Screening Tool ........................................................................................ 178

9.7.1

Size of the „included middle‟ .................................................................. 180

9.7.2

Micro/Macro-scale Conflicts of Interest ................................................. 181

9.7.3

Notes regarding Targets and Incentives .................................................. 184

9.8

The Condition Management Programme (CMP) ........................................... 185

9.8.1

Notes on „The Bio-psychosocial model‟ and „Illness Behaviour‟ .......... 186

9.8.2

Funding and Provision ............................................................................ 188

9.8.3

Some facts and observations ................................................................... 189

9.8.4

Return to Work Credit (RTWC).............................................................. 191

9.9

Section Summary and Discussion .................................................................. 198
Pathways to Work: Secondary Analysis of Early Quantitative Evidence of

Programme Effectiveness .............................................................................................. 204
10.1

Introduction: „The Graph‟ .............................................................................. 204

10.2

Inference ......................................................................................................... 208

10.2.1

The Concept of The Treatment Effect..................................................... 209

10.2.2

Controlling for Variables ........................................................................ 210

10.2.3

Comparisons ............................................................................................ 211

10.2.4

Changes over Time.................................................................................. 212

10.2.5

Combination ............................................................................................ 212

10.3

Inferring Treatment Effects from The Graph ................................................. 213

10.3.1

Axis Scales .............................................................................................. 215

10.3.2

The Rising Trend in Control Regions ..................................................... 216

10.3.3

Shifting Start Dates and Anticipatory Treatment Effects ........................ 217

10.4

Off-flow Rates and Number Needed to Treat ................................................ 218

10.4.1

Proxy Measures and Intended Outcomes ................................................ 220

10.5

Cost Per Successful Intervention: an illustrative approximation.................... 222

10.6

Aggregate Effects ........................................................................................... 224

10.6.1

Heavily Diluted Aggregate: Mixing „Treated Flow‟ with „Untreated

Stock‟

231

10.7

Introducing the Number Needed to Observe (NNO)...................................... 232

10.7.1

Estimating the Number Needed to Observe (NNO)................................ 233

10.8

Subsection Concluding Remarks .................................................................... 237

10.8.1

Concluding Observations Regarding The Graph: 6 Month verses 12

Month Off-Flow Rates ........................................................................................... 238

Pathways to Work: Secondary Analysis of Later Quantitative Evidence of

Programme Effectiveness .............................................................................................. 241
11.1

Introduction .................................................................................................... 241

11.2

The Policy Studies Report: Methodology ...................................................... 242

11.3

Reported Impact Estimates ............................................................................. 247

11.4

The Motivational Explanation and Employment Outcomes .......................... 252

11.5

Concluding Remarks ...................................................................................... 253


Discussions and Conclusion ............................................................................... 255

12.1

Introduction .................................................................................................... 255

12.2

The Five-year Discussion: Evidence-based politics and the rhetorical use of

statistics ..................................................................................................................... 256
12.2.1

Introduction ............................................................................................. 256

12.2.2

Redefining Purpose and Success ............................................................. 256

12.2.3

Bait and Switch ....................................................................................... 264

12.2.4

Discussion ............................................................................................... 266

12.3

The Thirty-year Discussion: On the nonlinear relationship between „job

fitness‟ (including physical fitness) and employability ............................................. 270
12.3.1

Introduction ............................................................................................. 270

12.3.2

The Model ............................................................................................... 271

12.3.3

Objections and Provisos .......................................................................... 275

12.3.4

Concluding Remarks ............................................................................... 278

12.4

The Hundred-year Discussion ........................................................................ 280

12.4.1

Introduction ............................................................................................. 280

12.4.2

The Second Epidemiological Transition, Ageing, and Retirement ......... 280

12.4.3

Concluding Remarks: Fair Retirement Ages and Incapacity Benefits .... 288

12.5

Policy Recommendations ............................................................................... 289

12.5.1

Hundred Year Recommendation: Flexible Entry to the Third Age ........ 289

12.5.2

Thirty Year Recommendation: Sheltered Employment .......................... 291

12.5.3

Five Year Recommendation: Technocracy and dispersal of power ........ 294

12.6

Summary and Discussion ............................................................................... 298

12.6.1

Interpretive Summary of the First Half of The Thesis ............................ 299

12.6.2

Theory and Government.......................................................................... 305

12.6.3

Government and Meaning ....................................................................... 307

Appendix: The Screening Tool ..................................................................................... 309
Glossary......................................................................................................................... 312
Bibliography .................................................................................................................. 314


List of Figures
Figure 1.1 Relationship between thesis sections and chapters ........................................ 18
Figure 1.2 Relationship between theory chapters ........................................................... 20
Figure 1.3 Relationship between evidence chapters ....................................................... 21
Figure 2.1 Levels of Life Regulation .............................................................................. 31
Figure 3.1 Two 'modes of living'. ................................................................................... 42
Figure 3.2 Mergers and Acquisitions. ............................................................................. 55
Figure 4.1 Milgram's Obedience to Authority experiments: Predicted break-points...... 64
Figure 4.2 Actual, predicted, and chosen shock levels ................................................... 66
Figure 5.1 Graphical representation of Charles Tilly's 'codes' ........................................ 95
Figure 7.1 International Labour Organisation classifications ....................................... 115
Figure 8.1 'Reasons for Inactivity' ................................................................................. 138
Figure 8.2 Number of people on benefits, 1979-2005 .................................................. 139
Figure 8.3 IVB/IB and ILO Unemployment levels within UK, 1975-2007 ................. 140
Figure 8.4 Working age population and IVB/IB claimant numbers, 1975-2007 .......... 142
Figure 8.5 IVB/IB claimants per 1000 people of working age, 1975-2007 .................. 143
Figure 8.6 Numbers of male IVB/IB claimants and female working age economic
activity rates .................................................................................................................. 144
Figure 8.7 Female IVB/IB claimants per 1000 male IVB/IB claimants, 1975-2007 .... 145
Figure 8.8 Index of IVB/IB population to overall working population, for males,
females, and combined (1975=100) .............................................................................. 146
Figure 8.9 'Inactivity rates for men with high levels of qualifications [A levels and
above] by age group and birth cohort; 1986 to 2001‟ (Original image in colour) ........ 147
Figure 8.10 'Inactivity rates for men with no qualifications by age group and birth
cohort; United Kingdom; 1986 to 2001' (Original image in colour) ............................ 148
Figure 8.11 Proportions employed according to socioeconomic group, 1975-2003 .... 150
Figure 8.12 Proportion employed according to limiting longstanding illness and
socioeconomic group..................................................................................................... 151
Figure 8.13 Proportions unemployed according to limiting longstanding illness and
socioeconomic group..................................................................................................... 152
Figure 8.14 Proportions inactive according to limiting longstanding illness and
socioeconomic group..................................................................................................... 153
Figure 8.15 Suggested relationship between economic activity, personal health and
economic 'health' ........................................................................................................... 156

Figure 8.16 Reasons for economic inactivity amongst males by age group (Original
figure in colour) ............................................................................................................. 158
Figure 8.17 Reasons for economic inactivity amongst females by age group (Original
figure in colour) ............................................................................................................. 158
Figure 8.18 Numbers of IB claimants by gender and age group, May 2007 ................ 160
Figure 8.19 Over- and under-representation of IB claimants at different age groups .. 161
Figure 9.1 Theory Underlying Screening Tool ............................................................. 180
Figure 9.2 RTWCs as a proportion of initial WFIs ....................................................... 195
Figure 9.3 RTWCs as a proportion of recorded job entries .......................................... 197
Figure 10.1 Six Month Off-flow Rates (Blyth 2006) .................................................... 205
Figure 10.2 'IB outflow in Pathways pilot districts compared with non-Pathway[sic]
districts‟ ......................................................................................................................... 206
Figure 10.3 „Pathways six month off-flow rate by month of benefit start and pilots
phase‟ (Original image in colour) ................................................................................. 207
Figure 10.4 '"Pathways to work" increases the off-flow from incapacity benefit (Six
month off-flow rate, per cent)‟ (Original image in colour) Source and title: Figure 3 of
OECD (2005) „Economic Survey of the United Kingdom, 2005‟, retrieved 3 July 2009
from http://www.oecd.org/dataoecd/18/34/35473312.pdf ............................................ 207
Figure 10.5 '"Pathways to Work" increases the off-flow from incapacity benefit'
(Original image in colour) ............................................................................................. 208
Figure 10.6 Examples of the importance of scale ......................................................... 216
Figure 10.7 Total number of IB/SDA claimants by claimant type, August 1999 - May
2007 ............................................................................................................................... 226
Figure 10.8 Total number of IB claimants (including IB Credits but excluding SDA) by
duration of claim, August 1999 - May 2007 ................................................................. 227
Figure 10.9 Proportion of all IB claimants (including IB credits, excluding SDA) who
have been on IB for 5 years or over, August 1999 - May 2007 ................................... 228
Figure 10.10 Trends in IB numbers by duration of claim, August 1999 - May 2007,
indexed to November 2003 (Pathways Pilot) levels ..................................................... 229
Figure 10.11 Trends in IB numbers by type of claim, August 1999 - May 2007, indexed
to November 2003 (Pathways Pilot) levels ................................................................... 230
Figure 10.12 New IB claimants (duration of 3 months and under), and Pathways starts,
August 2005- May 2007 ................................................................................................ 232
Figure 10.13 New IB claimants (duration of 6 months and under), and Pathways starts,
November 2005- May 2007 .......................................................................................... 234

Figure 10.14 New claimants (6 months and under) as a proportion of all claimants,
including Pathways region contributions ...................................................................... 235
Figure 10.15 '12 Month off-flow rate' Source: Slide 21 of DWP (2007), „Pathways to
Work: Invitation to Tender Provider Event‟, retrieved 14 May 2007 from
www.jobcentreplus.gov.uk/JCP/stellent/groups/jcp/documents/websitecontent/dev_013
465.ppt ........................................................................................................................... 239
Figure 11.1 'Tests of the counterfactual estimates of incapacity benefits receipt in
October 2003 areas in the period one year before the introduction of Pathways' ......... 244
Figure 11.2 'Tests of the counterfactual estimates of incapacity benefits receipt in
October 2003 areas in the period two years before the introduction of Pathways' ....... 244
Figure 11.3 'Tests of the counterfactual estimates of incapacity benefits receipt in April
2004 areas in the period one year before the introduction of Pathways' ....................... 245
Figure 11.4 'Tests of the counterfactual estimates of incapacity benefits receipts in April
2004 areas in the period two years before the introduction of Pathways‟ .................... 245
Figure 11.5 'Tests of the counterfactual estimates of incapacity benefits receipt in April
2004 areas in the period one year before the introduction of Pathways for those starting
a claim immediately prior to the introduction of Pathways' ......................................... 247
Figure 11.6 Estimated impact of Pathways treatment on four mutually exclusive
outcomes........................................................................................................................ 248
Figure 11.7 'Impact of Pathways on being off incapacity benefits by month, 2004 areas'
....................................................................................................................................... 249
Figure 12.1 Observed and latent variables .................................................................... 258
Figure 12.2 Graphical representation of empirical/imaged and observed/not observed
object distinction ........................................................................................................... 259
Figure 12.3 Example of imagined object as generative rule ......................................... 260
Figure 12.4 Three measurable indicators supporting the assertion that an 'aspirational
statement' is valid .......................................................................................................... 261
Figure 12.5 Pathways Evaluation: Original pairing of technical and aspirational
assertions ....................................................................................................................... 262
Figure 12.6 A recursive process leading readers to infer that more empirical objects
exist in support of an imagined object than are officially stated ................................... 263
Figure 12.7 Revised argument for the 'success' of Pathways ........................................ 264
Figure 12.8 Example of job selection process with two candidates for four jobs
(Original image in colour) ............................................................................................. 272


Figure 12.9 Predicted relationship between job 'fitness', number of rivals, and
employability................................................................................................................. 274
Figure 12.10 Male mean and variance of age of death, England and Wales, 1841-2005
....................................................................................................................................... 281
Figure 12.11 Female mean and variance of age of death, England and Wales, 1841-2005
....................................................................................................................................... 281
Figure 12.12 Male mean age of death and state retirement age, England & Wales, 18412005 ............................................................................................................................... 283
Figure 12.13 Male state retirement age as a 'changing promise' ................................... 285
Figure 12.14 Estimates of 'Fair' retirement ages based on social class ......................... 287


List of Tables
Table 3.1 Jared Diamond's 'Types of Societies' .............................................................. 46
Table 4.1 Qualitative interpretation of results to different types of Milgram experiment
......................................................................................................................................... 70
Table 5.1 Charles Tilly's 4 types of reasons .................................................................... 97
Table 6.1 Relationship between terms used by Polanyi & Fiske .................................. 109
Table 8.1 Pairwise correlation coefficients, employment, inactivity, and unemployment,
given social class and health, 1973-2003 inclusive....................................................... 155
Table 9.1 Key Incapacity Benefit events, according to the DWP ................................. 164
Table 9.2 Pathways evaluations as of 19 March 2008 .................................................. 170
Table 9.3 'Gains to be realised by those moving into work from an incapacity benefit
whilst the return-to-work credit is payable ................................................................... 193
Table 10.1 Six month off-flow rate estimates, control & treatment regions ................. 219
Table 10.2 Six month off-flow Number Needed to Treat estimates ............................. 219
Table 10.3 Pathways NNT estimates for employment outcome estimates, assuming 54%
'conversion' in phase one and 57% in phase two regions .............................................. 221
Table 10.4 Pathways NNT estimates for employment outcome estimates, assuming 52%
'conversion' in all regions ............................................................................................. 222
Table 11.1 'Effect of Pathways on receipt of incapacity benefits - summary measures for
April 2004 areas', with additional NNT estimates ........................................................ 250
Table A.1 The Screening Tool ...................................................................................... 310


Acknowledgements
Progress towards completing this thesis has been approximately sigmoid in shape: slow
in the first year, increasingly fast in the second year ("I think you‟re about six months
from completion..."), and then slow again in the third year ("...maybe not."). So slow, in
fact, that the third year stretched over into a fourth year as, well, „other stuff happened‟.
Sigmoid curves are asymptotic, never quite reaching their destination. I want to
apologise to my thesis advisory „team‟ -- Roger Burrows, Danny Dorling, and Kate
Pickett -- for at times making the sigmoid curve look more like prophecy than
approximation. I also want to thank Roger and Danny for allowing me the opportunity
to begin the PhD in the first place, and for then allowing it to develop (mutate?) from a
GIS project to something closer to a work of applied political philosophy.
I would also like to thank Stuart Adam, Jenny Allen, Kelly Benneworth, Beverly
Geesin, Roger Bowles, Jonathan Bradshaw, Gill Callaghan, Colin Campbell, Stephen
Clayton, Thomas Cochrane, Jacqueline Davidson, Richard Dorsett, Paul Drew, JeanBaptiste Gouyon, Steve Hall, Martin Ham, Nicola Holt, Gary King, Richard King,
Graham Lewis, Daryl Martin, Duncan McVicar, Rob Mellows, Dan Mercea, Andrew
Oswald, Theo Papadopoulos, Simon Parker, Nicholas Pleace, Darren Reed, Barbara
Robertson, Roy Sainsbury, Simon Shaw, Phil Stanworth, Charles Tilly, Merran Toerien,
Emma Uprichard, Roberto de Vogli, Simon Walker, Gemma Warren, Richard
Wilkinson, Astrid Winkler, and Simon Winlow. Each of these people, I think, has
contributed towards my thinking about the concepts, facts, and ideas developed over the
course of the PhD. They, of course, deserve some of the credit but none of the blame for
what is presented here, which they may disagree with entirely.
Finally, I would like to thank my mother, Helen Minton, for eagle-eyed proofreading,
and to her and my father, Nigel Minton, for allowing me to soliloquise at length to them
about many of the ideas contained within this thesis, even where such soliloquies were
unsolicited; and to thank Natalie Wheatley, also for proof-reading, and for offering
persistent encouragement that I "complete and submit it", despite not really knowing
what „it‟ is.


Author's Declaration
I hereby declare that the material in this thesis is solely my own and has not previously
been published.
